b' Money Stuff
I think there is a possible endgame now for the Sculptor Capital Management Inc. situation?%3Cp%3EWe%20have%20discussed%20this%20situation%2

Sculptor

I think there is a possible\xc2\xa0endgame now for the Sculptor Capital Management Inc. situation? [1] It goes like this:

  1. Right now, Sculptor has a deal to sell itself to Rithm Capital Corp. for $11.15 per share. And it has a competing bid from a group of hedge fund managers led by Boaz Weinstein, who want to buy Sculptor for something like $13 per share. Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s board of directors\xc2\xa0has, so far, refused to chuck the Rithm deal for Weinstein. Presumably the board hoped that Rithm would raise its price to something closer to $13.
  2. But after thinking about it for a bit, Rithm declined to raise its price .
  3. Sculptor could still ask its shareholders to vote to approve the Rithm deal. Last Thursday, it filed another draft proxy statement for that vote, recommending that shareholders vote for the $11.15 Rithm deal.
  4. Will they,\xc2\xa0though? Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s stock closed yesterday at $11.97. If you think that $11.15 is a good price, you should sell your stock, today, to someone who doesn\xe2\x80\x99t. Anyone who owns the stock by the time of the shareholder vote is going to want more than $11.15. [2]
  5. Meanwhile, Weinstein can\xe2\x80\x99t do anything to force Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s board to deal with him. In particular, as part of Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s original efforts to auction itself last year, Weinstein signed a nondisclosure agreement with a standstill clause barring him from making public offers to buy Sculptor. Traditionally the way to bypass the board is with a tender offer: You just go out and offer to buy everyone\xe2\x80\x99s shares for cash, and if more than half of the shareholders tender\xc2\xa0then you control the company. [3] But Weinstein can\xe2\x80\x99t do that, because of his NDA.
  6. But Weinstein\xe2\x80\x99s\xc2\xa0standstill expires on Dec. 22. It seems unlikely that Sculptor will be able to push through the Rithm deal before that. (There is no date set yet for the shareholder vote.)\xc2\xa0Which means that Weinstein might be able to launch a tender offer and buy the company.

This is not an optimal endgame. Weinstein would much rather come to a negotiated deal with Sculptor before December. For one thing, it would be faster. For another thing, tendering blindly to buy Sculptor without reaching any agreement with the board or management is risky . Weinstein is trying to buy\xc2\xa0an asset manager with some $30 billion or so of assets under management, much of it not very locked up, and that\xe2\x80\x99s a better deal if the clients plan to stick around. If he can talk to Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s management, meet with the biggest clients, hear their concerns and reassure them, that reduces the risk that he\xe2\x80\x99ll end up buying Sculptor and getting nothing. [4] There are regulatory and business risks to buying an asset manager with a tender offer, and it would not be anyone\xe2\x80\x99s first choice. [5]

But Weinstein probably has the ability to force that endgame, which means that he is in a better negotiating position to get the deal he wants. He can go to Sculptor and say:

  • Talk to me now, let me meet with your biggest clients and reassure them, and if all goes well we can sign a merger agreement at $13 per share in cash.
  • If you don\xe2\x80\x99t, you\xe2\x80\x99re still not going to get the Rithm deal \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0shareholders will vote no \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and I\xe2\x80\x99ll do a tender offer in December. But it won\xe2\x80\x99t be for $13. The delay and annoyance will cost your shareholders something, because I\xe2\x80\x99ll only pay $12.25 in the tender. I\xe2\x80\x99ll still get the company \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0$12.25 is more than $11.15, and more than it\xe2\x80\x99ll be worth with no deal \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but I\xe2\x80\x99ll pay less than I would if you negotiate now.

So that\xe2\x80\x99s what he did. Last Thursday\xe2\x80\x99s proxy statement lays out Weinstein\xe2\x80\x99s ask: He offered to sign an agreement to buy Sculptor for $12.76 per share, conditional on 40% of its hedge-fund clients consenting to the change in management, but he also offered some sweeteners. For one thing, if Sculptor lets him talk to its estranged founder, Dan Och, and the other former executive managing directors who still own a big chunk of Sculptor, he might raise his bid:

The September 30 Proposal reiterated the Consortium\xe2\x80\x99s request to contact the Former EMD Group and indicated that, depending on the outcome of its discussions and the Former EMD Group\xe2\x80\x99s willingness to enter into a rollover, the Consortium would consider increasing its price to $13.00 per share of Class A Common Stock.

For another thing, if Sculptor lets him talk to its clients , he might lower the client consent condition:

The Consortium noted that it may have the ability to further lower or eliminate the 40% client run-rate consent threshold for the Company\xe2\x80\x99s Multi-Strategy and Opportunistic Credit business lines if it were permitted to speak with certain of the Company\xe2\x80\x99s clients that comprise a substantial portion of the revenue associated with those strategies.

So if Sculptor negotiates with Weinstein and all goes well, it could get a deal at $13 per share without much conditionality. (Of course, all might not go well: If Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s board is really convinced that all of its big clients will say no to Weinstein, letting him talk to them will not get him to pay $13. It would get him to go away, though! Which also has some value.)

But the proxy also lays out Weinstein\xe2\x80\x99s threat about what will happen if the board doesn\xe2\x80\x99t negotiate:

The Consortium further stated an intention to initiate a cash tender offer for a reduced price of $12.25 per share of Class A Common Stock following the expiration of Bidder J\xe2\x80\x99s standstill on December 22, 2023, if the Company Stockholders had not approved the Merger Proposal prior to such time.

It\xe2\x80\x99s a good threat: $12.25 is less than $13 (or $12.76), but more than $11.15. If you are a Sculptor shareholder, asked to approve the $11.15 Rithm deal, you will be tempted to say no and hold out for the $12.25 Weinstein tender. [6] Which means there\xe2\x80\x99s a good chance that the Rithm deal will fail and Weinstein will end up buying the company at $12.25. Which means that, for the board, it would be better to get $13 from Weinstein now than $12.25 from him later.

Meanwhile the board was working on a process in which:

  • Rithm would raise its price, maybe not to $12.76 but to something closer than $11.15, [7] and
  • in exchange, Och and the other former executives would agree to support the Rithm deal, further freezing out Weinstein. [8]

That would have been a good outcome for the board: They get the deal they are comfortable with, and that they think clients will be comfortable with, but at a price that is good enough\xc2\xa0for shareholders. But that didn\xe2\x80\x99t work out, mostly because Weinstein raised his bid and Och decided Weinstein was better than Rithm, so Rithm \xe2\x80\x9cwas not prepared to (1) enter into the amended Merger Agreement without the Former EMD Support or (2) offer any other improvements to the terms and conditions of the Merger Agreement or the Transactions.\xe2\x80\x9d

And so Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s board, which has been pretty tough about ignoring Weinstein so far, has opened the door a crack. It\xe2\x80\x99s writing a merger agreement to send to Weinstein:

So as not to lose time while the Special Committee was evaluating whether to make a Superior\xc2\xa0Proposal Determination, the Special Committee authorized Latham & Watkins to request Rithm\xe2\x80\x99s consent to engage with the Consortium and instructed Latham & Watkins to begin preparing revised draft definitive transaction documents to send to the Consortium that the Special Committee would be prepared to authorize at a price of $13.00 per share of Class A Common Stock.

And it\xe2\x80\x99s not saying no:

The Special Committee determined to continue to evaluate the September 30 Proposal with its advisors and continue to engage with Rithm regarding potential improvements to the terms and conditions of the Merger Agreement and the Transactions in parallel.

The Special Committee is continuing to evaluate the September 30 Proposal, as amended by the October 2 Letter, in consultation with its independent financial and legal advisors and will make its recommendation to the Company\xe2\x80\x99s Board of Directors and determine next steps in due course. Consistent with its fiduciary duties, the Special Committee and the Company\xe2\x80\x99s Board of Directors will determine the best path forward with the interest of maximizing value for all Company Stockholders.

It seems like Weinstein has worn the board down enough that he might get his deal. Or, of course, the board has worn Weinstein down enough that they might get $13 per share. Arguably this is a win for both of them.

Deposit duration

We have talked a lot around here about two theories of banking.

  1. Banks borrow short to lend long. Banks get their funding from deposits, which can be withdrawn at any time, and use it to buy long-term loans and bonds. This is risky. If interest rates go up, banks have to pay more for funding, while the value of their long-term assets goes down.
  2. Banks borrow long to lend long. Bank deposits can be withdrawn at any time, but\xc2\xa0they mostly aren\xe2\x80\x99t . Banks invest in long-term relationships with customers, who deposit money and are not inclined to withdraw it. And banks use that cheap long-term funding to buy long-term assets, and also to invest in the things (branches, customer service, Little League sponsorship ) that create the long-term relationships.

Theory 1, I have said, is the standard theory of modern capital markets; Theory 2 is the traditional theory of banking . Theory 2 more or less describes how most banks mostly work; Theory 1 more or less describes why Silicon Valley Bank failed this spring.

You could have intermediate theories. Theory 1 says that bank deposits are short-term, Theory 2 that they are long-term. Here is \xe2\x80\x9c Deposit Convexity, Monetary Policy, and Financial Stability ,\xe2\x80\x9d by\xc2\xa0Emily Greenwald and\xc2\xa0Sam Schulhofer-Wohl of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas\xc2\xa0and Joshua Younger of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, which argues that deposits are long-term, but become shorter-term as rates go up. Which is exactly what you don\xe2\x80\x99t want:

In principle, bank deposits can be withdrawn on demand. In practice, depositors tend to maintain stable balances for long periods, allowing banks to fund long-dated assets. Nevertheless, the cost of deposit funding influences banks\xe2\x80\x99 capacity for maturity transformation. Banks and researchers conventionally model the response of deposit interest rates to market interest rates as constant, implying that deposits have nearly constant duration. Contrary to this standard assumption, we show empirically that the \xe2\x80\x9cbeta\xe2\x80\x9d of deposit rates to market rates increases as market rates rise, causing the duration of deposits to fall. The amount of duration risk delivered to bank balance sheets via this channel from March 2022 to September 2023 is comparable in magnitude to the amount of duration risk absorbed by each of the several large-scale asset purchase programs the Federal Reserve has undertaken since 2008. Dynamic betas present a significant challenge to bank portfolio hedgers by introducing large and dynamic risks that are difficult to model and impractical to replicate on the asset side of the balance sheet. As a result, deposit convexity amplifies monetary policy transmission and increases financial fragility, mechanisms that recent banking stresses have highlighted.

Intuitively, banks match the duration of their assets (mortgages, Treasury bonds, etc.) to the duration of their liabilities (deposits, assuming that the deposits are pretty stable). As interest rates go up, the assets lose value: A 10-year Treasury bond with 3% interest is worth 100 cents on the dollar when rates are 3%, but only 85 cents when rates are 5%. But as interest rates go up, the value to the bank of its deposit franchise should also go up: Paying 0% on stable deposits is worth more when rates are 5% than when they are worth 3%. The books balance. Except that this is wrong, and in reality\xc2\xa0the duration of the deposits goes down as rates go up.

Elsewhere in banking :

Banks globally reaped a $280 billion profit boost in 2022 thanks to rising rates, in the sector\xe2\x80\x99s best performance since the 2008 global financial crisis, McKinsey & Co. said.

An unprecedented series of interest-rate increases by monetary authorities across the world has provided the biggest tailwind for the global banking industry in over a decade. The bonanza has prompted several lenders to announce billions of dollars in share buybacks.

When Silicon Valley Bank failed, a lot of people asked, in effect, \xe2\x80\x9chow could they have been so stupid?\xe2\x80\x9d The math was obvious: They had a bunch of long-term bonds funded with a bunch of short-term deposits; as rates went up, the bonds lost value and the deposits fled. A standard Theory 1 sort of objection. And the answer was, roughly: \xe2\x80\x9cActually banks usually benefit from rising rates, and SVB bought all those long-term bonds in part because it was worried about the risk of rates going down , which would crush its profitability.\xe2\x80\x9d A standard Theory 2 type of answer. Which was wrong about SVB, but right in general: The thing that blew up SVB and a handful of other US regional banks [9] was also \xe2\x80\x9cthe biggest tailwind for the global banking industry in over a decade.\xe2\x80\x9d

It\xe2\x80\x99s not just SVB though. Bloomberg\xe2\x80\x99s Katherine Doherty reports :

For years, Brian Moynihan told investors his bank would be a big winner when interest rates eventually, finally, one day went up. Bank of America Corp. had built a massive deposit base that would allow profits to soar when the Federal Reserve changed course.

Rates have indeed shot up \xe2\x80\x94 and BofA is the biggest laggard among the US banking giants. Its investments earn less than its closest rivals, and the stock is the group\xe2\x80\x99s worst performer by far.

In part, that\xe2\x80\x99s because BofA piled hundreds of billions of dollars into long-dated Treasuries and mortgage bonds at low rates that prevailed during the pandemic. The decision is still the subject of finger-pointing within the walls of the second-largest US bank, say people familiar with the matter, now that those holdings are showing huge paper losses and missing out on some of the best rates since 2007.

If your thesis is \xe2\x80\x9cwe have built a massive deposit base that will allow profits to soar when the Fed changes course,\xe2\x80\x9d your main risk is that rates will stay low and profits won\xe2\x80\x99t soar. You hedge that risk by buying long-dated Treasuries: If rates go up, you lose money on your Treasuries but you\xe2\x80\x99re happy because of your deposit base; if they don\xe2\x80\x99t, you make (relatively) a lot of money on the Treasuries to tide you over. (Doherty: \xe2\x80\x9cThe move was designed to help protect the bank\xe2\x80\x99s net interest margin if rates didn\xe2\x80\x99t move or declined.\xe2\x80\x9d) But if (1) everyone else doesn\xe2\x80\x99t hedge that risk, and keeps their money in short-term assets and (2) the Fed does rapidly raise rates, then you will look comparatively bad.

Closed-end muni fund duration

Outside of banking, meanwhile, Theory 1 is basically correct? That\xe2\x80\x99s why people were so puzzled by SVB\xe2\x80\x99s failure: Everywhere else in capital markets, it is just obvious that if you borrow short-term to buy long-duration assets, and rates go up, you will lose money. Banks are special because their borrowing is technically short-term but really something else. But lots of other businesses are in this sort of maturity transformation business without that special advantage. The Wall Street Journal reports today :

Some municipal-bond funds are suffering their worst stretch since the 2008-09 financial crisis, an acute example of how two years of rising interest rates have slammed investors\xe2\x80\x99 portfolios.

Closed-end municipal-bond funds have been particularly hard-hit because they often use borrowed money to invest in fixed-rate, long-term bonds sold by state and local governments. The leverage helps boost the returns from debt that is ultrasafe, but pays relatively little interest.

That worked for much of the past decade until rates started rising. Now, short-term borrowing is becoming more expensive while the market value of older, lower-yielding bonds in the mutual funds is falling.

Yes right if you use $50 of your own money and $50 of borrowed money to buy a $100\xc2\xa0bond\xc2\xa0that pays 2% interest, and your borrowing costs 0%, then you make a 4% return on your $50. If your borrowing costs 5% and the bond\xc2\xa0still pays 2%, you lose money.

Coin flips

We have talked , several times now, for reasons, about whether and how much you should bet on a coin flip with slightly positive expected value. One claim about the Jane Street Capital internship program, which gave Sam Bankman-Fried his start in financial markets, is that \xe2\x80\x9ca\xc2\xa0Jane Street intern had what amounted to a professional obligation to take any bet with a positive expected value,\xe2\x80\x9d very much including a biased coin flip with 1% edge.

Well, bad news for Jane Street interns: That\xe2\x80\x99s every coin flip. Here is a new paper titled \xe2\x80\x9c Fair coins tend to land on the same side they started: Evidence from 350,757 Flips ,\xe2\x80\x9d by Franti\xc5\xa1ek Barto\xc5\xa1 and a surprisingly long list of co-authors:

Many people have flipped coins but few have stopped to ponder the statistical and physical intricacies of the process. In a preregistered study we collected 350,757 coin flips to test the counterintuitive prediction from a physics model of human coin tossing developed by Persi Diaconis. The model asserts that when people flip an ordinary coin, it tends to land on the same side it started -- Diaconis estimated the probability of a same-side outcome to be about 51%. Our data lend strong support to this precise prediction: the coins landed on the same side more often than not,\xc2\xa0Pr(same side)=0.508, 95% credible interval (CI) [0.506,\xc2\xa00.509],\xc2\xa0BFsame-side bias=2364. Furthermore, the data revealed considerable between-people variation in the degree of this same-side bias. Our data also confirmed the generic prediction that when people flip an ordinary coin -- with the initial side-up randomly determined -- it is equally likely to land heads or tails:\xc2\xa0Pr(heads)=0.500, 95% CI [0.498, 0.502], BFheads-tails bias=0.183. Furthermore, this lack of heads-tails bias does not appear to vary across coins. Our data therefore provide strong evidence that when some (but not all) people flip a fair coin, it tends to land on the same side it started. Our data provide compelling statistical support for Diaconis' physics model of coin tossing.

Actually the list of co-authors is so long because they needed people to flip the coins. \xe2\x80\x9cA group of 48 people (i.e., all but three of the co-authors) tossed coins of 46 different currencies \xc3\x97 denominations and obtained a total number of 350,757 coin flips,\xe2\x80\x9d says the paper . Academia is great.

The paper\xc2\xa0does\xc2\xa0not describe the technique\xc2\xa0that \xe2\x80\x9csome (but not all) people\xe2\x80\x9d use to flip a coin to cause it to land more often on the starting side. Nor are there diagrams. [10] Nonetheless I assert:

  1. There is probably a technique.
  2. It is probably learnable.
  3. If you get good at it you can probably get your probability up to, like, 55%.
  4. If you have a Jane Street internship next summer you had better start practicing now.

Things happen

Exxon to Buy Pioneer for $60 Billion to Dominate Shale Oil. JPMorgan Has a Master Plan to Beat Competitors in Silicon Valley.\xc2\xa0KKR and Carlyle Take No Carry on New Private Credit Funds. Birkenstock Prices IPO at $46 a Share. Metro Bank Gets Bondholder Backing for Debt Restructuring. US agencies broaden crackdown on \xe2\x80\x98 junk fees .\xe2\x80\x99\xc2\xa0Mallinckrodt Bankruptcy Plan Gets Approval, Will Wipe Out $1 Billion in Opioid Payments . Hamas Militants Behind Israel Attack Raised Millions in Crypto .\xc2\xa0Crypto Sector Seeks Lawyers , Compliance Officers After Reputational Hits.

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] We have discussed this situation a lot around here. We first discussed the Weinstein consortium bid here , and the basic economics of it here . We discussed Dan Och's interests here , Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s board\xe2\x80\x99s concerns here and here , and the most recent developments here .

[2] Except maybe Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s management, led by Chief Executive Officer Jimmy Levin, who have their own reasons for preferring Rithm, and who have agreed to vote for the deal. But they own roughly one-fifth of the votes and can\xe2\x80\x99t force the deal through on their own.

[3] This is oversimplified, and in theory the board could use a poison pill to block him, but I think in this situation \xe2\x80\x94 where they\xe2\x80\x99ve agreed to sell to Rithm for $11.15 in cash \xe2\x80\x94 it's tough to block a higher tender.

[4] Or, of course, if he meets with all the clients and they say \xe2\x80\x9cwe hate you and would pull our money immediately,\xe2\x80\x9d then he knows not to go through with the deal. In a tender he can\xe2\x80\x99t really do that; in a negotiated deal he can do due diligence before signing, and if the diligence on this point is bad he can just not sign.

[5] The proxy hints at these concerns: \xe2\x80\x9cThe Special Committee further noted that any proposed tender offer by the Consortium would be subject to U.S. tender offer rules, and it was unclear to the Special Committee how the Consortium would be able to secure required regulatory approvals and client consents in connection with a tender offer made directly to the Company Stockholders. Following discussion, the Special Committee continued to have significant concerns as to whether the Consortium Client Consent Condition could reasonably be expected to be satisfied, and therefore whether the proposed transaction with the Consortium could reasonably be expected to close.\xe2\x80\x9d

[6] To be clear: There is no $12.25 Weinstein tender. It\xe2\x80\x99s just a thing he said in a letter. If you are a Sculptor shareholder voting no on the Rithm deal, it\xe2\x80\x99s because you think it is highly likely that Weinstein will follow through with this, because you think he really wants the company. But it is a risk. If you think he\xe2\x80\x99s just pulling Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s leg, you should take the $11.15. (Or, I mean, sell your stock at $11.97 now.) Pulling Sculptor\xe2\x80\x99s leg would be *odd* behavior, but you can\xe2\x80\x99t really rule it out. If Weinstein blows up the Rithm deal and then *doesn\xe2\x80\x99t* buy Sculptor, then arguably he has crippled a competitor. Plus there are weird personal dynamics here, and you can\xe2\x80\x99t put the odds of some sort of personal grudge motivation at zero.

[7] As part of raising its price, Rithm also wanted a higher termination fee. This is reasonable, and the board was okay with it, but it is a bit odd. The board was willing to give Rithm a higher termination fee, to protect its deal from being jumped by Weinstein, in exchange for Rithm raising its price to *a price lower than Weinstein\xe2\x80\x99s*. If Rithm had raised to $13.10, sure, give it a fully locked-up deal. But raising to $12.20, when there\xe2\x80\x99s a competing bid at $12.76 or so, does not really justify a higher breakup fee. Anyway they never signed it so this is moot.

[8] One other factor here is that Rithm\xe2\x80\x99s deal still has a condition that 85% of clients have to consent. Given that the board keeps saying the Weinstein deal is too risky because it requires 80% of real-estate and collateralized-loan-obligation clients to consent, and 40% of hedge-fund clients, it is awkward that its preferred deal with Rithm requires 85%. The board can say that it is confident clients would consent to Rithm and not Weinstein, but it\xe2\x80\x99s odd to have a double standard in the formal document. It was trying to fix that in the revised Rithm deal: \xe2\x80\x9cFollowing a discussion, the Special Committee determined that it would ask Rithm to eliminate the Rithm Client Consent Condition in the Merger Agreement. \xe2\x80\xa6 On September 23, 2023, representatives of Citi informed representatives of J.P. Morgan that assuming the Special Committee was otherwise prepared to agree to the other Proposed Amendments (and subject to receiving the Former EMD Support), Rithm would agree to remove the Rithm Client Consent Condition in the Merger Agreement.\xe2\x80\x9d But then they never signed the amendment.

[9] And \xe2\x80\xa6 Credit Suisse Group AG? Indirectly?

[10] There is, however, a table of the co-authors\xe2\x80\x99 individual results, which range from 48.7% to an astounding 60.1% (1,682 of 2,800, a 95% confidence interval of 58.2% to 61.9%) of flips landing on the same side. Tianqi Peng of the University of Amsterdam could make a killing flipping coins at Jane Street.
